figures.gloss: The ubiquitous, sovereign 'Lady' of the palatial records, often invoked with local or functional epithets ('Potnia of the Labyrinth', 'Potnia of the Horses'). She receives massive state allocations of perfumed oil, honey, and textiles.
figures.facet: She represents an overwhelming, polyvalent matrix of divine female power. The bureaucratic records blur the line between one universal Goddess and a fractured chorus of local Ladies, long before they hardened into the distinct Olympians.
figures.provenance: Found across Mycenaean sites; prominently at Knossos (KN Gg 702) and Pylos (PY Tn 316).

figures.gloss: The preeminent male deity of the Mycenaean palatial system, particularly at Pylos. He is heavily associated with the earth, subterranean springs, horses, and massive state-sponsored blood sacrifices.
figures.facet: Unlike his classical incarnation who was relegated to the sea and subordinated to Zeus, the Bronze Age Poseidon is the supreme, terrifying lord of the earth's depths, the primary sovereign to whom the palace answers.
figures.provenance: Extensively documented in the Pylos archives (e.g., PY Un 718, PY Tn 316).

figures.gloss: The inherited Indo-European sky god, actively worshipped but frequently appearing as a secondary figure behind Poseidon and Potnia in palatial offering lists. He is closely tied to the calendrical cycle.
figures.facet: He has not yet achieved the undisputed, patriarchal supremacy of Mount Olympus. He operates as just one high-ranking node in a massive administrative network, sharing power with a fiercely matriarchal pre-Greek religious substratum.
figures.provenance: Knossos (KN Fp 1) and Pylos offering tablets.

figures.gloss: A distinct female deity whose name is the grammatical feminine of Zeus. She possesses her own sanctuary (the Diwion) and receives discrete offerings alongside the sky-father.
figures.facet: She demonstrates a rigid, Bronze Age theological duality where major male gods are mirrored by linguistic feminine counterparts, a balanced pairing that was erased when the classical Hera absorbed the role of divine wife.

figures.gloss: A deity securely attested in the Bronze Age archives, receiving offerings of honey and wine. His presence shatters the older scholarly consensus that he was a late, foreign interloper to Greece.
figures.facet: He is not a wild outsider crashing the classical pantheon from Thrace or the East, but an indigenous, primal force of vegetative life and ecstatic rupture deeply embedded in the earliest bureaucratic heart of Greek religion.
figures.provenance: Khania (KH Gq 5) and Pylos (PY Xa 102) Linear B tablets.

figures.gloss: An ancient, chthonic goddess who enforces oaths and natural boundaries, recorded in the archives receiving standardized palatial offerings of olive oil.
figures.facet: Before multiplying into the classical chorus of shrieking Furies, Erinys is venerated as a singular, sovereign goddess of the earth's unyielding order, fully integrated into the state cult rather than exiled as a pure curse-demon.

figures.gloss: The supreme palatial overlord, functioning simultaneously as a secular king and the central ritual intermediary. He is woven into the very ledgers of divine offerings, receiving and redistributing the sacred wealth of the state.
figures.facet: He anchors a theology of accounting. His religious authority does not stem from heroic monster-slaying, but from his capacity to audit and guarantee the massive, logistical flow of sheep, oil, and bronze to the gods.
figures.provenance: Ubiquitous in Pylos and Knossos Linear B archives; survives in Homeric Greek as 'anax'.

figures.gloss: Attested in the tablets receiving offerings, possibly with a martial epithet (the a-re-ja reading is debated). He is deeply connected to boundaries, transitions, and the distribution of sacred goods.
figures.facet: Unlike his later incarnation as a purely fleet-footed messenger, the Bronze Age Hermes has martial and deeply chthonic associations, acting as an enforcer of thresholds in a highly militarized palatial society.
figures.provenance: Pylos and Knossos tablets (e.g., PY Tn 316, KN D 411).

figures.gloss: A goddess recorded receiving offerings in the same Pylos sanctuary entry as Zeus (Tn 316), alongside gold and bronze vessels; not yet cast as jealous wife or fixed Olympian queen, simply a major, independently named cult recipient paired with the sky-father.
figures.facet: Her Bronze Age role is functional pairing, not marital narrative: the domestic warfare between Zeus and Hera in later myth is a literary accretion layered onto what the tablets show as a straightforward joint cult.
figures.provenance: Pylos tablet PY Tn 316; John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

figures.gloss: A deity attested at Knossos (a-re) receiving cult offerings and recognizable as a theophoric element in Bronze Age personal and place names.
figures.facet: War is a palatial cult category before it is a poetic embarrassment: Ares belongs to the same administrative offering system as Zeus and Poseidon, centuries before Homer's disreputable Ares.
figures.provenance: Knossos tablets (KN Fp 14, KN X 5951); John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

figures.gloss: A named goddess recorded at Pylos (a-te-mi-to / a-ti-mi-te) receiving her own allocations, distinct from the Potnia entries elsewhere in the same archive.
figures.facet: She arrives in the record already individuated by name, before the classical inheritance of Potnia Theron's hunting title folds wild-animal dominion into her — huntress and Lady of Beasts are, in the Bronze Age, still two separate threads.
figures.provenance: Pylos tablets (PY Es 650, PY Ea 421); John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

figures.gloss: A healer god recorded at Knossos (pa-ja-wo) on the same tablet as Erinys, receiving cult offerings independently of any other god; Apollo himself is absent from the entire Linear B corpus.
figures.facet: The Bronze Age healer stands alone, later swallowed into Apollo's cult as the mere epithet and hymn-refrain 'Paean' — a full god demoted to a vocative address.
figures.provenance: Knossos tablet KN V 52; John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

figures.gloss: Birth goddess of the Amnisos cave near Knossos, recorded receiving an amphora of honey sent to her sanctuary at Amnisos.
figures.facet: Hers is one of the cleanest text-to-site-to-classical-cult continuities in Aegean religion: the same goddess, the same cave, largely the same offering register, running from Minoan through Mycenaean into Homeric religion (Odyssey 19.188).
figures.provenance: Knossos tablet KN Gg 705; the Eileithyia Cave at Amnisos, excavated by Spyridon Marinatos (1929).

figures.gloss: A goddess recorded at Pylos (po-si-da-e-ja) whose name is the grammatical feminine of Poseidon, receiving her own share of offerings in the Tn 316 series.
figures.facet: She is the clearest casualty of classical religion's simplification of the Bronze Age pantheon: a fully attested cult partner of one of the two most powerful Mycenaean gods, erased so completely that no trace survives into any later Greek source.
figures.provenance: Pylos tablet PY Tn 316; John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

figures.gloss: A goddess recorded at Pylos (i-pe-me-de-ja) among the Tn 316 offering recipients, surviving in Homer and later myth only as a mortal woman, mother of the giants Otos and Ephialtes.
figures.facet: She documents demotion in the most literal sense the corpus can show: the same name moves from a divine offering-list to a mortal genealogy within the space of a few centuries.
figures.provenance: Pylos tablet PY Tn 316; John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

figures.gloss: 'Drimios the son of Zeus', recorded at Pylos among the Tn 316 recipients receiving offerings alongside Zeus, Hera, and Poseidon, and then vanishing from every later Greek source.
figures.facet: He is the sharpest single piece of evidence that the Bronze Age pantheon was not a rough draft of the Olympians: a named son of Zeus with a state cult, and no myth, epithet, or classical successor at all.
figures.provenance: Pylos tablet PY Tn 316; John Chadwick, 'The Mycenaean World' (1976).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The supreme sovereign Lady and the supreme Lord of the Earth, forming the unacknowledged dyad of Bronze Age palatial power.
Intra-tradition: The perfect grammatical and theological mirroring of the Indo-European sky-father with his native feminine counterpart.
GOLD SEAM: Direct, reconstructed linguistic and functional descent from the Proto-Indo-European sky-father.
GOLD SEAM: The direct Bronze Age ancestor of the classical Greek king of the gods.
GOLD SEAM: The absolute, terrifying dominion over the subterranean depths, a role Poseidon held before the classical mythos split the realm and handed it to Hades.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign goddesses of supreme authority whose cults are entirely integrated into massive, centralized bureaucratic redistributive economies.
GOLD SEAM: The indisputable Bronze Age attestation of the wine god, proving he is indigenous to Greek religion rather than a later foreign import.
GOLD SEAM: The Knossos tablets explicitly record offerings to 'a-ta-na po-ti-ni-ja' (Athena Potnia), the direct link to the classical virgin goddess of the citadel.
GOLD SEAM: Classical Artemis inherits the Bronze Age title 'Potnia Theron' (Lady of the Beasts), retaining the ancient sovereign dominion over wild spaces.
GOLD SEAM: The theological impulse to perfectly balance a supreme male principle with a direct, nominal feminine counterpart.
GOLD SEAM: Primordial, feminine personifications of cosmic and natural order who predate and bind the authority of the anthropomorphic gods.
GOLD SEAM: The human sovereign functioning strictly as a ritual vessel and conduit for divine resources, bound by exhaustive ceremonial duties.
GOLD SEAM: The direct evolution of the Bronze Age boundary-enforcer into the classical psychopomp and messenger.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign lords of the deep, earthly waters and foundational cosmic oaths who stand in tension with the active, lightning-wielding sky deities.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, localized Bronze Age goddesses who hold ultimate state authority, to whom the earthly kings directly answer and provide massive tribute.
GOLD SEAM: The fundamental, unyielding law of the cosmos that enforces oaths and punishes transgression, preceding the whims of individual gods.
GOLD SEAM: Dying-and-rising gods heavily associated with liquid life (wine, the Nile) who anchor the deepest, most archaic layers of their respective traditions.
Direct Bronze Age continuity: classical Poseidon inherits the Linear B po-se-da-o's name and evidently much of his cult standing wholesale.

both preserve an older layer in which a god's power over horses — Poseidon Hippios in his Mycenaean cult, the Despotes Hippon in Iberian reliefs — precedes or stands apart from any later maritime or civic elaboration.
Intra-tradition: joint cult recipients in the same Pylos Tn 316 sanctuary entry.
Intra-tradition: the exact grammatical and theological mirroring of Poseidon, parallel to the roster's existing Zeus/Diwia pair.
Intra-tradition: both are erased feminine counterparts of major gods (Zeus/Diwia, Poseidon/Poseidaeia), showing the pattern is systemic across the Bronze Age pantheon, not an isolated case.
Intra-tradition: Tn 316 explicitly records him as 'Drimios the son of Zeus.'
Intra-tradition: both recorded together on the same Knossos tablet, KN V 52 — direct tablet co-occurrence.
the direct Bronze Age ancestor of the classical goddess, matching the roster's existing pattern for mycenaean-dionysus and mycenaean-hermes.

dossier.essence: The religion of the palace-centered societies of Bronze-Age Greece (c. 1600–1100 BCE), reconstructed almost entirely from Linear B administrative tablets rather than any myth or theology. It shows a crowded divine world, worshipped through a redistributive palace economy, whose membership overlaps with but does not match the later Olympians — the earliest documented layer of Greek religion.
dossier.cosmos: The Linear B corpus is bureaucratic and records who received offerings, not what was believed, so Mycenaean cosmology, myth, and eschatology are largely unknown. What the tablets reveal is a pantheon in which a sovereign goddess (or goddesses) called Potnia, 'the Lady', looms very large alongside Poseidon (foremost at Pylos), di-we (Zeus), Hera, and figures including Dionysus, Hermes, Ares, and a paired Diwia. Beliefs about death must be inferred from archaeology — tholos and chamber tombs and rich grave goods such as the Shaft Graves at Mycenae — rather than from any surviving text.
dossier.practice: Cult was administered through the palace: scribes logged allocations of perfumed oil, grain, wine, honey, spices, and sacrificial animals to sanctuaries and named deities, as in the great Pylos offering-tablet PY Tn 316. A wanax (king) stood at the apex with cultic as well as political functions, served by priests and priestesses (i-je-re-u, i-je-re-ja) who held land and office. Worship centered on shrines, sanctuary buildings, and communal sacrificial feasting attached to the redistributive economy.
dossier.canon: No scripture, myth, or narrative survives; the sources are the Linear B tablets themselves (from Pylos, Knossos, Thebes, Mycenae, and Tiryns), read alongside the archaeology of shrines, frescoes, and tombs.
dossier.reflects: Religion visible only as accountancy — a cult recoverable strictly through the audited flow of commodities to the gods, sacred bookkeeping standing in for any surviving belief.
sources: Martin P. Nilsson, 'The Minoan-Mycenaean Religion and Its Survival in Greek Religion' (1950)
Michael Ventris & John Chadwick, 'Documents in Mycenaean Greek' (1956)
